item.title: Anchovy fillets
item.description: Salt-cured Engraulis encrasicolus fillets in olive oil. The Mediterranean kitchen's primary glutamate-and-IMP umami source, used as background savory builder rather than fish flavor.

item.title: Apple cider vinegar
item.description: Acetic-acid solution (5 to 6 percent) produced by acetobacter fermentation of apple cider. The default American baking and pickling acid, with residual apple-fruit notes and a softer profile than wine vinegars.

item.title: Avocado
item.description: Persea americana, fruit of the avocado tree, dominated commercially by the Hass cultivar. Cold-pressed monounsaturated fat in a creamy parenchyma; ripens off the tree, browns on cut surfaces from polyphenol oxidase activity, and provides structural fat in raw Mexican and Latin American cooking.

item.title: Bacon
item.description: American-style smoked, sugar-brined pork belly. Structural rendered-fat-and-smoke base under American breakfasts, German warm potato salads (Schwäbischer Kartoffelsalat), New England chowders, BLTs, and any dish where smoke-and-sweet pork crisp is the load-bearing flavor.

item.title: Basil
item.description: Ocimum basilicum. The defining summer herb of Mediterranean and Southeast Asian cuisines. Volatile-oil-rich (linalool, eugenol, methyl chavicol), heat-fragile, oxidation-prone, a finishing herb in nearly every application.

item.title: Black beans (frijoles negros)
item.description: Small to medium black-skinned legumes (Phaseolus vulgaris cv. Black Turtle) with a creamy off-white interior. The structural legume of central Mexican, Cuban, and Brazilian cooking; rich in soluble fiber, anthocyanin pigments in the seed coat, and the highest free-glutamate of any common bean.

item.title: Blood orange
item.description: Citrus × sinensis cultivars (Tarocco, Moro, Sanguinello) whose flesh accumulates anthocyanin pigments under cool-night growing conditions. Crimson-streaked, more acidic than common navel, with raspberry-and-bitter-orange aromatics.

item.title: Capers
item.description: Unopened flower buds of Capparis spinosa, salt-packed or brined. The Mediterranean kitchen's primary mustard-pungent finishing pickle, dense in glucosinolates that release on chewing.

item.title: Catfish (channel, basa, swai)
item.description: Freshwater bottom-feeding finfish in several closely related families, 6 to 12% fat by weight, with a firm dense white flesh, a thin skin that crisps when seared, and a characteristic mild flavor with a slight earthy back-note from sediment-feeding biology. The structural fish of the US South, the Mississippi delta, the Mekong delta, and Bangladeshi and West African catfish traditions.

item.title: Chaat masala
item.description: North Indian finishing spice blend built around kala namak (sulfurous Himalayan black salt) and amchur (dried unripe mango powder), with toasted cumin, coriander, ajwain, ginger, and chili. The signature sulfur-sour-saline axis defines its identity; not interchangeable with garam masala.

item.title: Cherry tomato
item.description: Small (1.5 to 3 cm) round or oval tomato cultivars (Solanum lycopersicum), bred for high sugar concentration, dense pectin-rich flesh, and intense aromatic compounds relative to slicing tomatoes. The structural sweet tomato of salads, pastas, and pounded salads where the cook wants tomato gel and sweetness without the mass and water of a slicing tomato.

item.title: Chipotle in adobo
item.description: Smoke-cured ripe jalapeños (chipotle morita or chipotle meco) rehydrated in a tomato-vinegar-aromatic sauce. The most concentrated form of Mexican smoke heat in a single pantry can; carries pectin and tomato emulsifier body that makes it function as a vinaigrette base, not just a flavoring.

item.title: Chives
item.description: Allium schoenoprasum, the smallest of the cultivated alliums. Hollow grass-thin green leaves, mild fresh onion aromatic, used raw or barely-heated as a finishing herb across French sauce work, German potato salads, Eastern European sour cream applications, and any dish where allium brightness is wanted without the volume or pungency of onion.

item.title: Cod
item.description: Atlantic or Pacific cod (Gadus morhua, G. macrocephalus). Mild, flaky, low-fat (0.7 percent) white-fleshed groundfish; the canonical lean white fish of the North Atlantic. Cooks in a narrow window because of the very low fat content. The default firm-white-fish substitute when halibut is unavailable, and the structural protein of fish-and-chips, brandade, bacalao, and Iberian salt-cod traditions.

item.title: Cotija
item.description: Aged Mexican cow-milk cheese from the highlands of Michoacán and Jalisco. Hard, dry, salt-forward, intensely lactic; the canonical finishing-and-grating cheese for elote, esquites, beans, and roasted-vegetable salads. Often called "the Parmesan of Mexico" though closer in microstructure to a young pecorino or ricotta salata.

item.title: Cucumber
item.description: Cucumis sativus. A 95-percent-water cucurbit grown for cool, crisp flesh and edible skin; the kitchen workhorse for raw salads, quick pickles, and yogurt sauces.

item.title: Culantro (ngò gai, sawtooth herb)
item.description: Long sawtooth-edged herb (Eryngium foetidum) in the same family as cilantro but with a more intense, slightly bitter aromatic profile carrying the same dodecenal compound as cilantro at 3 to 5× concentration. The defining herb of Caribbean sazón, Vietnamese pho and canh chua, and several Southeast Asian sour-soup traditions.

item.title: Daikon (radish)
item.description: Long white winter radish (Raphanus sativus var. longipinnatus), 90 to 95% water by weight, with active myrosinase enzymes that convert intact glucosinolates into pungent isothiocyanates the moment cell walls are ruptured. The structural ingredient of oden, daikon mochi, kkakdugi, banh mi do chua, and the Japanese oroshi (raw grated radish) condiment.

item.title: Dijon mustard
item.description: Smooth pale-yellow mustard from Dijon, France: brown mustard seeds (Brassica juncea) ground in verjuice or white wine. Pungent, mucilaginous, the canonical French emulsifier-and-acid in vinaigrettes.

item.title: Dried oregano (Mediterranean)
item.description: Dried leaves of Origanum vulgare, the Mediterranean oregano. Carvacrol- and thymol-dominant, peppery and slightly camphoraceous; the canonical Italian-American pizza herb and a quiet structural backbone in tomato sauces and Greek cooking.

item.title: Dried shrimp
item.description: Small whole shrimp salt-cured and sun-dried to 10 to 15% moisture, sold loose or in sealed plastic bags. A concentrated source of glutamate and inosinate that defines the savory backbone of Thai, Vietnamese, Cambodian, southern Chinese, and West African cooking.

item.title: Dry white wine (for cooking)
item.description: Fermented grape wine, white, dry (residual sugar under 4 g/L), 11 to 13 percent alcohol, with acidity at 6 to 8 g/L (tartaric). The default acid-aromatic deglaze and braising liquid in French, Italian, and Spanish cooking; not "cooking wine," which is salted and degraded.

item.title: Dungeness crab
item.description: Pacific brachyuran crustacean, Metacarcinus magister, harvested live from Alaska to central California. Sweet, large-flaked white meat plus rich brown mustard (the hepatopancreas); the body meat is the canonical ingredient under San Francisco cioppino and West Coast crab boils.

item.title: Farro
item.description: Whole-grain wheat (most often Triticum dicoccum, emmer) sold in three pearling grades. Chewy, nutty, with a structural starch that holds shape after long cooking. The default Italian whole grain.

item.title: Fennel bulb
item.description: Florence or Sweet fennel, Foeniculum vulgare var. dulce. Crisp white-green basal bulb (swollen leaf bases, not a true bulb) with anise-perfumed fronds and feathery green stalks. Anethole-dominant, with parsley and tarragon undertones; the canonical bulb of Mediterranean fish cookery.

item.title: Fennel seed
item.description: Dried ripe fruit of Foeniculum vulgare. Greenish-brown ribbed seeds, intensely anise-scented from trans-anethole concentration of 30 to 50 mg per gram. The defining spice of Italian sweet sausage and a quiet structural element in seafood broths and South Asian spice blends.

item.title: Feta
item.description: Greek brined sheep's-milk cheese (PDO since 2002), made from at least 70 percent sheep's milk with up to 30 percent goat. Crumbly, lactic-tangy, salt-cured in brine for 2 to 3 months. The defining cheese of Greek and broader Eastern Mediterranean cooking.

item.title: Glutinous rice (sticky rice, sweet rice)
item.description: Short- to medium-grain rice (Oryza sativa, glutinous cultivars) whose endosperm is essentially pure amylopectin. Cooks to a translucent, chewy, fully cohesive mass with no individual-grain separation. The table rice of Laos, northeast Thailand, and parts of Yunnan; the structural rice of mochi, zongzi, sticky-rice cakes, and toasted-rice powder.

item.title: Gochugaru (Korean chili flakes, 고추가루)
item.description: Sun-dried Korean red chili (Capsicum annuum, taeyangcho cultivar) milled to flakes or fine powder. Mild to medium heat (1,500 to 10,000 Scoville), bright red color, rounded warm aromatic profile. The chili-flake top note across Korean cooking; structural in kimchi, oi-muchim, ojingeo bokkeum, and tteokbokki.

item.title: Greek yogurt
item.description: Whole-milk yogurt strained through cloth to remove a substantial fraction of the whey. Concentrated protein (10 percent), thicker texture, tangier flavor than American-style yogurt. Structural ingredient in dips, sauces, and Eastern Mediterranean cooking.
